danger_signals.visual_form: Single exclamation mark icon appearing above soldier's head
danger_signals.meaning: Enemy has spotted player; local alert confined to current screen

danger_signals.visual_form: Two exclamation marks above enemy head (intended feature, unused in NES)
danger_signals.meaning: Higher alert phase indicating reinforcements will pursue across multiple screens

danger_signals.visual_form: Screen-wide alert state triggered by camera detection, infrared sensor, or loud noise
danger_signals.meaning: Reinforcements spawn off-screen; player must eliminate enemies or escape to adjacent area/floor/outdoor

danger_signals.visual_form: Gunshot sound effect and visual muzzle flash
danger_signals.meaning: Creates noise that triggers alarm and full Alert Mode with reinforcements

danger_signals.visual_form: Animated soldier sprites moving along predetermined paths
danger_signals.meaning: Visual contact with these entities triggers Alert Mode; can be neutralized with fists or weapons

safety_signals.visual_form: Moving to adjacent screen removes enemy pursuit
safety_signals.meaning: Single-exclamation alerts are automatically canceled when changing screens; safe to resume infiltration
Escapes Alert Mode; provides safe passage between floors

safety_signals.visual_form: Visual transition to outdoor jungle/perimeter environment
safety_signals.meaning: Exiting building removes active Alert state and pursuing reinforcements

safety_signals.visual_form: Empty corridor or room with no animated guard characters
safety_signals.meaning: Safe to move and explore without immediate detection risk

safety_signals.visual_form: Handgun or submachine gun with suppressor attachment equipped
safety_signals.meaning: No noise generated; safe to eliminate guards without triggering alarm

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: FOXHOUND military unit insignia (implied through radio communications)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Player character is unmarked soldier; can equip enemy uniform to blend in
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Radio transceiver as communication device; presence in base as infiltrator

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Guard soldiers in uniform sprites; distinct from player character model
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Military base architecture (Buildings 1, 2, 3, 5); patrolling soldiers; security doors; key card locks
Radio frequencies for contact (Big Boss, Schneider, Diane, Jennifer); rescued prisoners represent resistance sympathizers

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Player sprite moves freely; guards patrol in predictable patterns; no alert indicators visible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies perform standard patrol routines; will only investigate if player enters line of sight

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Exclamation mark appears above detecting enemy; screen-wide enemy activation visible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: All enemies on current screen attack; switching screens cancels alert and resumes normal patrol

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Double exclamation marks; reinforcement soldiers spawn and pursue across multiple screens
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Off-screen reinforcements chase player relentlessly until all enemies eliminated or player reaches safe area

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Rank stars increase on HUD; carrying capacity visually expands in inventory; max health increases
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Every 5 rescued hostages = rank increase; dead hostage causes rank demotion; affects equipment slots available

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Locked door sprite with keycard requirement indicator; visual barrier blocks passage
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must obtain corresponding key card; doors open only with matching card or enemy uniform disguise

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Weapon/equipment icons appear in inventory screen with distinct visual designs (gun, missile launcher, grenade, suit, flashlight)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: New capabilities unlock (e.g., suppressor silences shots; bomb suit protects from hazards; RC missiles bypass obstacles)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Electric panels, heat panels, or deep water shown as distinct environmental visual obstacles
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Certain equipment required to traverse (bomb blast suit for electrified floors, RC missiles to destroy obstacles)

environmental_storytelling.example: Buildings 1, 2, 3, 5 with multiple floors; each area locked behind progressively higher-tier key cards (card 1, 2, 3); visual door states tell player where they can and cannot access
Defeated/freed prisoners provide story exposition and mission objectives; each rescue advances rank and unlocks new capabilities, creating narrative momentum through mechanical progression

Player must track four different ally frequencies (Big Boss, Schneider, Diane, Jennifer); each character provides contextual advice based on current location and equipment, revealing world state through dialogue

environmental_storytelling.technique: Equipment discovery as world state indicator
environmental_storytelling.example: Finding suppressor, gas mask, binoculars, bomb blast suit in specific locations tells player about environmental hazards and enemy security measures ahead
Three main buildings with distinct floor layouts and elevator systems; northern vs. southern entrances; basement areas; visual design communicates strategic importance and guard positions
Mercenary bosses (Twin Shot, Coward Duck, Fire Trooper, Grey Fox) serve as visual embodiment of enemy faction strength and military capability; each boss represents different tactical approach

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Moves in fixed patterns across screen; ignores player if not in line of sight
Displays exclamation mark; attacks player; alerts other guards on screen
Spawn off-screen and pursue relentlessly across adjacent screens until eliminated or player escapes to safe zone
Can move freely when undetected; can equip enemy uniform to bypass certain guards
Must either eliminate all enemies, flee to adjacent screen/floor/outdoor, or enter combat encounter
Remains stationary in cell; rescued when player reaches location; each rescue increases rank
Initiates forced combat scenario; uses specialized tactics and weapons; victory yields equipment or access to new areas
Visual contact triggers immediate Alert Mode with reinforcements; cannot be avoided without detection
Invisible threat that triggers Alert Mode if player passes through; requires equipment or evasion to bypass

notes: NES version significantly differs from original MSX2 version. Higher alert phases (double exclamation marks with cross-screen reinforcement pursuit) were designed for MSX2 but removed in NES port, as alert state resets when changing screens in NES version. Regional variants include: UI polish on weapon icons, transceiver design changes, title screen publisher logos (Ultra Games vs. Konami), and level layout alterations. Enemy uniform mechanic allows visual faction disguise but is less critical to progression than in MSX2. Radio transceiver communication system (Big Boss, Schneider, Diane, Jennifer) is primary narrative mechanism for world-state exposition.
